name: Flask
what_it_does: Lightweight Python web microframework providing URL routing, request handling, templating (Jinja2), and a development server. Flask is intentionally minimal — adds only what's necessary for web development. Extensions add functionality (Flask-SQLAlchemy, Flask-Login, Flask-RESTful). Popular for REST APIs, ML model serving, and small-to-medium web applications.
use_cases: Build simple REST APIs for ML model serving or data microservices with minimal framework overhead
Create web applications with Jinja2 templates where Django's full stack is unnecessary
Prototype web services quickly with Flask's minimal setup (no ORM, no auth built-in by default)
Serve LLM or ML models as HTTP APIs in research or production environments
Build webhooks receivers and simple API endpoints without framework ceremony
not_for: Large applications needing built-in admin, auth, and ORM — use Django for batteries-included approach
High-performance async APIs — FastAPI is async-first with better performance and automatic OpenAPI docs
Applications requiring real-time WebSocket support — use FastAPI or Django Channels
best_when: You need a simple, flexible Python web framework for REST APIs, ML serving, or lightweight web apps without Django's full stack.
avoid_when: You need async performance, automatic API documentation, or built-in auth/admin — FastAPI or Django are better choices.

auth.notes: Framework with no built-in auth. Add Flask-Login, Flask-JWT-Extended, or custom auth middleware.

pricing.notes: Free and open source, maintained by the Pallets project.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — self-hosted. WSGI synchronous by default; use with Gunicorn for production.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Flask's debug mode provides Werkzeug interactive debugger with variable inspection. Production errors return JSON or HTML based on Accept header. Error handlers registered with @app.errorhandler customize responses.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Route handlers follow HTTP idempotency semantics — developer responsibility.
known_agent_gotchas: Flask's development server is NOT suitable for production — use Gunicorn, uWSGI, or Uvicorn (for async) in production; dev server is single-threaded and unsafe
Flask application context (app_ctx) and request context (req_ctx) are thread-local — code outside request handling (background threads, CLI commands) must push app context explicitly
FLASK_ENV and FLASK_DEBUG environment variables are deprecated in Flask 2.x — use FLASK_DEBUG=1 directly; app.run(debug=True) for development
Flask-SQLAlchemy requires db.session.commit() or db.session.rollback() to be called explicitly — uncommitted sessions are rolled back at request end but not persisted
Blueprint url_prefix must be specified when registering — forgetting url_prefix causes all blueprint routes to be at root URL, conflicting with app routes
Flask-CORS is required for cross-origin requests — browsers block cross-origin requests without CORS headers; CORS is NOT configured by default in Flask

security_score_components.security_notes: Flask doesn't include CSRF protection — add Flask-WTF for CSRF. SECRET_KEY required for session security. Input validation is developer responsibility — no built-in validation. XSS protection via Jinja2 auto-escaping.
